pathophysiology_summary: Solar erythema (sunburn) is UV-induced epidermal injury and inflammation producing delayed (4–6h) uniform, hot, tender, blanching erythema over sun-exposed skin with a sharp clothing demarcation; a first-degree burn has intact dry skin without blisters and heals with peeling over days. The ground-truth task is (1) depth assessment — blistering/weeping indicates a partial-thickness burn needing burn-type care, and extensive severe burns may warrant review — and (2) screening concurrent heat-related systemic illness (heat exhaustion/heat stroke: headache, confusion, nausea/vomiting, dizziness, very high temperature) and dehydration, which are the genuine dangers. Management is NSAID + paracetamol, cool compresses/emollient, hydration and strict sun avoidance.

differential.evidence_basis: Delayed uniform hot tender blanching erythema over sun-exposed skin with a tan line, no blisters.
Blisters/weeping would reclassify as deeper needing burn-type care.
Screen systemic heat-illness features after prolonged sun exposure.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Superficial sunburn leads; deeper burn and heat illness must be screened before symptomatic reassurance.

differential.evidence_basis: No blisters, no heat-illness features, well hydrated — first-degree sunburn for symptomatic home care.
No blistering; escalation target if blisters develop over 24–48h.
No systemic heat-illness features; escalation target if they develop.

differential_progression.scoring_note: After a negative depth/heat-illness screen, superficial sunburn leads; NSAID/paracetamol + cool care + hydration + sun avoidance.

differential.evidence_basis: Working diagnosis; peaks ~24h, peels over days; reassess if it blisters.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Final position is benign first-degree sunburn.

red_flags.description: Partial-thickness / extensive burn — blistering, weeping, or very extensive severe burn
red_flags.clinical_significance: Deeper/extensive burns need burn-type wound care and possibly review.

red_flags.description: Heat exhaustion / heat stroke / dehydration — severe headache, confusion, nausea/vomiting, dizziness/faintness, very high temperature, not passing urine
red_flags.clinical_significance: Heat stroke is a life-threatening emergency.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Ice causes further injury; use cool (not icy) compresses and bland emollient.
Not screening systemic heat-related symptoms after prolonged sun exposure.

telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: NSAID + paracetamol + cool compress/emollient + hydration + sun avoidance; ED for blistering/extensive burn or heat-illness features.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Missing heat stroke or a deeper burn.

guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: First-degree sunburn: cool compresses, bland emollient, NSAID + paracetamol, hydration, strict sun avoidance. Blistering/extensive burns need burn-type care; consider heat-related illness with systemic symptoms.
